coredata.dc:title: Green carbon dots are produced hydrothermally for the detection of PA in water environments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Caihong
Li, Haichao
Zhou, Meng
Chen, Miaomiao
Wu, Chunchao
Zuo, Han
Zhang, Zhe
Pang, Shujie
Yang, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: This study used m-phenylenediamine and erythromycin in a one-step hydrothermal technique to synthesize carbon dots with green fluorescence emission to detect picric acid in an aqueous environment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were used to analyze the synthesized G-CQDs. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were used to determine the optical characteristics. In the linear range of 0–60 μM, G-CQDs demonstrated strong selectivity for picric acid, with a detection limit as low as 0.18 μM. Green fluorescent carbon dots were combined with polyvinyl alcohol to form a hydrophilic film responsive to picric acid. Combined with smartphone-based image analysis technology, this system establishes a rapid quantitative detection method. This portable detection approach features simple operation and low cost, providing a novel solution for on-site environmental pollutant monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Fluorescent
Detection of PA
Fluorescence composite film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of G-CQDs/PVA fluorescence composite film
Specific detection of picric acid by G-CQDs
Detection of picric acid by G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite film
Preparation of CD under optimized conditions
Application of G-CQDs in picric acid detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CQDs (b) Size distribution of (c) XRD pattern (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of CQDs (b) fluorescence spectra of CQDs with excitation wavelengths ranging from 370 nm to 450 nm in 10 nm increments (inset: photographs of CQDs under sunlight and UV–Vis irradiation).

(a) Fluorescence response images of G-CQDs with the addition of common metal ions and picric acid (b) histogram of fluorescence response intensity of G-CQDs with the addition of picric acid in the presence of other metal ions (c) fluorescence response images of G-CQDs with the addition of common aromatic compounds and picric acid (d) fluorescence response images of G-CQDs with the addition of picric acid in the presence of other aromatic compounds Fluorescence response intensity histogram.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of G-CQDs with different concentrations of PA added (b) scatter plots of fluorescence response of G-CQDs with different concentrations of PA added (inset is a linearly fitted image of the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs at PA concentrations of 0–60 μM) (c) images of G-CQDs with different concentrations of PA added under natural light (d) images of G-CQDs with different concentrations of PA under 365 nm UV light.

(a) Photographs of fluorescent films under natural and UV light with different concentrations of picric acid PA (0–1000 μM) added to G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite films (b) photographs under natural and UV light after 1-day and 20-day storage (c) schematic diagram of smartphone-assisted portable detection (d) the linear relationship of RGB values with PA concentrations from 0 to 100 μM.
Preparation of G-CQDs, synthesis of G-CQDs/PVA, and their detection for PA
Schematic representation of possible sensing mechanism.
Determination of PA in Nanhu Lake, Yitong River, and tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Green carbon dots are produced hydrothermally for the detection of PA in water environments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Public Experimental Center, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun 130117, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Public Experimental Center, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun 130117, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Kinlita Chemical Co., Ltd, Shanghai 201417, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Kinlita Chemical Co., Ltd, Shanghai 201417, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study used m-phenylenediamine and erythromycin in a one-step hydrothermal technique to synthesize carbon dots with green fluorescence emission to detect picric acid in an aqueous environment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were used to analyze the synthesized G-CQDs. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were used to determine the optical characteristics. In the linear range of 0–60 μM, G-CQDs demonstrated strong selectivity for picric acid, with a detection limit as low as 0.18 μM. Green fluorescent carbon dots were combined with polyvinyl alcohol to form a hydrophilic film responsive to picric acid. Combined with smartphone-based image analysis technology, this system establishes a rapid quantitative detection method. This portable detection approach features simple operation and low cost, providing a novel solution for on-site environmental pollutant monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Because nitroaromatic compounds have high explosive qualities, they are frequently used in producing products like paint, matchboxes, rocket fuel, and pyrotechni [1]. The international community is seriously threatened by the frequent occurrences of explosives misuse and illegal manufacturing, which has made the global security situation more complicated in recent years [2]. The majority of explosives comprise various chemicals, such as picric acid (PA), cyclortrimethylenetrinitramine (RDX), dinitrotoluene (DNT), and trinitrotoluene (TNT) [3]. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [4], ion mobility spectrometry [5], electrochemical methods [6], coordination polymer methods [7], Raman spectroscopy [8], and hydrogen-bonded organic framework methods [9] are now the most widely employed detection techniques. Nevertheless, few PAs have been found, as most explosives detection techniques have concentrated on electron-deficient polynitroaromatic analytes like DNT and TNT [10]. PA is a powerful organic acid that has an even greater explosive capability than TNT [11].PA synthesis is facilitated by phenol and nitro functional groups that are dissolved in groundwater or fall into the soil [12,13]. Prolonged human exposure to PA can cause severe skin and eye irritation, irreversible kidney damage, and respiratory immune system damage [14], which, in extreme cases, can be fatal. Therefore, the need for sensors that can swiftly and accurately detect picric acid is very significant.

Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) with a size under 10 nm are a novel material [15], because of their high fluorescence stability and low toxicity, Carbon dots have garnered much attention since their unintentional discovery in 2004 following the production of electrophoretically pure single-walled carbon nanotubes [16,17] Among the special functional characteristics of carbon quantum dots are their low cost, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, size/surface adjustable powerful photoluminescence (PL), high chemical stability and photostability [18,19]. Because of these unique characteristics, carbon dots have been found to have applications in anticounterfeiting [20], cellular imaging [21], nano-temperature monitoring [22], and other fields. Carbon dots may also be studied optically in a variety of methods, including upconversion fluorescent Carbon dots [23–25], long-life room temperature phosphorescence of Carbon dots [26–28], and fluorescence of electrons [29–31]. To do this, we will identify dangerous compounds in the environment by using the exceptional fluorescence capabilities of Carbon dots.

The hydrothermal technique of generating carbon dots has drawn a lot of attention due to its benefits, which include moderate reaction conditions [32], no need for extra passivation [33], simple access to reaction solvents [34], and inexpensive equipment [35]. However, the quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs reported in existing studies remain generally unsatisfactory. For instance, Cai et al. achieved only 16.7 % QY for nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) synthesized from pumpkin and melamine via hydrothermal method [36]. Liu et al. reported that fresh tomato-derived CQDs initially exhibited a mere 1.77 % QY, which only increased to 7.9 % and 8.5 % after modification with ethylenediamine or urea, respectively [37]. The CQDs prepared by Karthika Anpalagan's team using wheat bread, soybean flour, and lemon juice through the hydrothermal method showed an even lower QY of 2.31 % [38]. Additionally, while Li et al. obtained yellow fluorescent CQDs with 22.85 % QY from o-phenylenediamine and sulfamide via hydrothermal synthesis [39], and Wu's group achieved 13.9 % QY for blue fluorescent CQDs derived from silk with excellent photostability, these yields still leave room for improvement [40]. To address these limitations, this study optimized precursor selection and synthesis strategy by employing m-phenylenediamine and erythromycin as starting materials. Although erythromycin is more expensive than common amino acid precursors, the resulting green fluorescent CQDs (G-CQDs) achieved a remarkable QY of 25.87 %, significantly surpassing most reported hydrothermal-synthesized CQDs. The enhanced quantum yield and stability effectively reduce the required dosage in practical applications, thereby offsetting material costs and improving long-term operational efficiency. This work provides a novel approach for hydrothermal preparation of high-performance CQDs.

M-phenylenediamine and erythromycin were used as the raw ingredients for the hydrothermal approach used in this work to create Carbon dots (G-CQDs) with green fluorescence and a quantum yield of 25.87 %. With a detection limit of 0.18 μM, G-CQDs demonstrated excellent sensitivity and selectivity for PA in an aqueous environment. One of the best materials for creating hydrogels is polyvinyl alcohol, which is non-toxic, hydrophilic, and biocompatible. The hydrophilic G-CQDs/PVA luminous composite film with good tensile qualities was created by natural air drying after the produced G-CQDs were compounded with polyvinyl alcohol. In the presence of PA, the fluorescent composite film exhibits a burst phenomenon under 365 nm UV light, allowing for quick detection of PA in the aquatic environment since the G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite film also possesses exceptional luminous qualities.

m-Phenylenediamine, Erythromycin, Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA), silver nitrate, calcium nitrate, cobalt nitrate, chromium chloride, sodium dichromate, copper nitrate, ferrous sulphate, ferric chloride, mercuric chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium sulphate, manganese chloride, sodium chloride, nickel nitrate. Picric Acid (PA), 4-Methoxyphenol, 3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid, Potassium biphthalate, Aniline, Sulfanilic acid, Toluene, p-Nitrotoluene standard, Phthalic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, p-Aminophenol, p-Phthalic acid, Salicylic acid. All of the solvents and reagents were bought from commercial companies and utilized without additional purification. A Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany) was used to obtain ultrapure water for all studies.

Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA). Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrometer (Agilent, USA). An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan). A Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scien­tific, USA).

For the first step, 10 ml of deionized water was mixed with 0.0216 g of m-phenylenediamine and 0.073 g of erythromycin, which were then sonicated until fully dissolved. Following transferring the mixture to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave made of stainless steel, the reactor was heated to 160 °C for 6 h. Following the reaction, the solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm after being allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. Following centrifugation, a 0.22 μM membrane was used to filter the top layer of the cleared solution, yielding the G-CQDs solution, which was then refrigerated at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of G-CQDs/PVA fluorescence composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The first step involved weighing and stirring 3 g of polyvinyl alcohol in 90 °C deionized water. After pouring the mixture into a three-necked round-bottom flask and mechanically stirring it for 40 min at 90 °C in an oil bath, 10 mL G-CQDs were added, and the stirring continued for another 10 min. Once the mixture was homogeneous, it was moved to a beaker and cooled for 15 min. The beaker was placed in an ultrasonic cleaner to remove any remaining air bubbles. After pouring the defoamed G-CQDs and polyvinyl alcohol composite solution into the mold at room temperature, it was allowed to dry for 12 h naturally. Ultimately, the G-CQDs/PVA luminous composite film was dried for 12 h at 60 °C in an oven to eliminate any remaining moisture. The fluorescent G-CQDs/PVA composite films were produced. After removing the dried film, place it in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific detection of picric acid by G-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The exact concentration (100 μM) of PA, nitroaromatic compounds (4-Methoxyphenol, 3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid, Potassium biphthalate, Aniline, Sulfanilic acid, Toluene, p-Nitrotoluene standard, Phthalic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, p-Aminophenol, p-Phthalic acid, Salicylic acid), and metal ions Ag(I), Ca(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), Na(I), and Ni(II) in a standard solution were required to achieve the specific detection of PA by G-CQDs. Furthermore, the intensity of the fluorescence response at the ideal excitation wavelength of 400 nm, which corresponds to the optimal emission wavelength of 501 nm, was measured. To study PA's ability to prevent interference with CQDs when heavy metal ions and other nitroaromatic chemicals are present. Aqueous solutions of PA at varying concentrations (0–1000 μM) were set up to examine the sensitivity of CQDs to PA. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 501 nm emission wavelength and 400 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of picric acid by G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite film was treated with a specific concentration of PA solution. After allowing the reaction to proceed for 3 min, visible changes were observed and photographed using a smartphone. Subsequently, fluorescence images were captured under UV illumination and analyzed directly for RGB (red-green-blue) values using a smartphone application. The total RGB value was calculated using the following equation: RGB value = red + green+ blue (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD under optimized conditions
ce:section.ce:para: M-phenylenediamine and erythromycin were used as input ingredients for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Optimizing the ratios of raw materials, reaction solvents, and reaction time led to the optimal experimental circumstances. We examined the produced samples using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. We collected their fluorescence spectrum data after gradually varying the raw material ratios, reaction duration, and reaction temperature while maintaining the same experimental parameters. Fig. S1(a) and (b) can visualize that the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs reaches the highest when the molar mass ratio of m-phenylenediamine and erythromycin is 2:1. Under the same conditions, Fig. S1(c) and d) responds to changes in the experimental reaction time, with a maximum fluorescence intensity at an experimental period of 6 h. Fig. S1(e) and S1(f) When the reaction temperature is 160 °C, the optimal emission wavelength of G-CQDs can reach 501 nm, and the relative fluorescence intensity can reach its maximum. When the reaction temperature is too high or too low, the optimal emission wavelength is blue-shifted, and the fluorescence intensity associated with the optimal emission is lower than that of the reaction temperature of 160 °C. As a result, the following tests were carried out using the molar mass ratio of 2:1 between erythromycin and m-phenylenediamine, the hydrothermal reaction period of 6 h, and the reaction temperature of 160 °C.

Several TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and EDS tests were conducted to examine the carbon dots specifically and examine their nanostructures and characteristics. First, we used transmission electron microscopy to describe their morphology. The formed G-CQDs, which are displayed in Fig. 1(a), exhibit no crystalline structure and excellent dispersion. After counting and calculating the CDs' particle size, the findings are shown in Fig. 1(b). The average size of the G-CQDs was around 6.75 nm, with their sizes being dispersed roughly between 6.5 and 8.0 nm. The crystal structure of G-CQDs was described using X-ray diffraction. The XRD analysis in Fig. 1(c) revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.2°, corresponding to an amorphous carbon structure [41], and the TEM patterns support this assertion. The surface functional groups will be examined using FT-IR to describe the surface composition of the synthesized G-CQDs. According to Fig. 1(d), the broad peak at 3440 cm−1 is caused by an OH or NH stretching vibration [42], the CH stretching vibrational peaks are responsible for the peak at 2829 cm-1 [43], and the acute peak at 1612 cm−1 is thought to be produced by the overlap of CO and CC bonds [44]. The peak near 1109 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO bending vibration or the stretching vibration of the ester group [45], whereas the peak at 1364 cm−1 suggests the potential presence of CN or CC functional groups [46]. The presence of meso-substituted benzene structures is further supported by the peaks at 620 cm−1 and 710 cm-1 [47]. The G-CQDs were subjected to energy spectroscopy analysis to better ascertain their chemical composition. The EDS elemental distribution Fig. S2 clearly shows that the elements C, N, and O are present in the G-CQDs at 45.67 %, 14.22 %, and 40.11 %, respectively.

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs were examined at constant concentration. Fig. 2(a) displays the UV–Vis spectra of G-CQDs, which reveal two absorption bands at 288 nm and 345 nm, respectively. The π-π* jumps of the aromatic CC bond are represented by the peaks seen at 288 nm [48,49], whereas the n-π* leaps of CO are responsible for the absorption bands at around 345 nm [50]. The emission spectra of G-CQDs exhibit a typical excitation wavelength-dependent pattern, as seen in Fig. 2(b). The emission peaks of the G-CQDs exhibit a clear redshift in the 480–520 nm range and attain the maximum emission intensity at 501 nm when the excitation wavelength is increased from 350 nm to 450 nm. This indicates that 400 nm is the ideal excitation wavelength and 501 nm is the perfect emission wavelength for this carbon quantum dot. The complexity of the excited states on the surface of the G-CQDs is responsible for this excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior, which may be connected to both the variety of the surface states and the distribution of the various particle sizes [51,52]. Because of their various functional groups, the G-CQDs may have carbon quantum dot surfaces with varying surface morphologies and potential emission defects [53]. Emission flaws on the surface of G-CQDs may alter the emission wavelength and impact the emission behavior as the excitation wavelength increases. G-CQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior as a result of different emission wavelengths altering their fluorescence emission behavior [53]. In fluorescent carbon compounds, this is typical [54]. G-CQDs produce green fluorescence when exposed to UV light but yellowish fluorescence throughout the day, as seen in Fig. 2(b) inset. There is a 25.87 % quantum yield.

Stability is a crucial feature of fluorescence sensors for real-world uses. As a result, we created additional tests to investigate the fluorescence stability of CQDs. As shown in Fig. S3(a), G-CQDs at pH = 1–14 exhibit an ideal excitation wavelength of 400 nm, and the corresponding optimal emission peaks merely alter the fluorescence intensity without causing red-shifting or blue-shifting. Fig. S3(b) shows that the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs increased with a relatively small change in pH between 1 and 6, remained nearly unchanged between pH 7 and 10, and was suppressed and almost burst when pH exceeded 11 near the strong alkaline environment. Consequently, the ideal reaction pH for all further tests was pH = 6. Fig. S4(a) and (b) demonstrates that G-CQDs have strong salt resistance since their fluorescence intensity did not change much with varying salt concentrations. As shown in Fig. S4(c) and (d), G-CQDs exhibited good light resistance since their fluorescence intensity remained almost constant as the irradiation period under a 365 nm UV lamp increased. Under identical experimental conditions, we prepared five independent batches of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S4(e) and (f), the fluorescence intensity showed minimal variation across batches, confirming good reproducibility and stability of the synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of G-CQDs in picric acid detection
ce:section.ce:para: Adding various interfering chemicals and PA to G-CQDs separately and monitoring the change in G-CQD fluorescence intensity allowed researchers to ascertain the selectivity of G-CQDs for PA. Under ideal reaction circumstances, the metal ions were investigated to determine if they would affect picric acid detection. Additionally, we need to confirm if other comparable nitro-aromatic chemicals impact the selectivity of G-CQDs for PA, as PA is an aromatic nitro molecule.

In a diluted aqueous solution of G-CQDs, PA, nitroaromatic compounds (such as 4-Methoxyphenol, 3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid, Potassium biphthalate, Aniline, Sulfanilic acid, Toluene, p-Nitrotoluene standard, Phthalic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, p-Aminophenol, p-Phthalic acid, Salicylic acid), and common metal ions (such as Ag(I), Ca(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), Na(I), and Ni(II)) were added to a diluted aqueous solution of G-CQDs. The solution was then allowed to stand for 3 min to make sure the reaction was finished. At an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the emission wavelength of G-CQDs was measured. It is evident from Fig. 3(a) and (c) that, in contrast to the other metal ions, which did not considerably alter the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs, the addition of PA resulted in a considerable drop. Likewise, no discernible fluorescence burst was produced by other aromatic compounds. In summary, PA significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs. Furthermore, the detection of PA exhibited high selectivity and was unaffected by metal ions or other aromatic compounds.

The G-CQDs solution will then be mixed with PA, other metal ions, and aromatic compounds. It will then be left for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity at the ideal excitation wavelength will be measured after the process is finished. As can be seen from the data in Fig. 3(b) and (d) below, even when other ions or substances are interfering with the fluorescence of G-CQDs, the bursting impact of PA on that fluorescence remains considerable. Thus, the G-CQDs that we produced had strong detectability in the presence of metal ions or other compounds, as well as good selectivity for picric acid.

To investigate the role of erythromycin in enhancing the detection sensitivity of carbon quantum dots toward PA. As shown in Fig. S5, we carried out a control experiment where carbon dots synthesized without erythromycin (mPD-CQDs) and carbon quantum dots synthesized with erythromycin and m-phenylenediamine (G-CQDs) were added to picric acid (PA) solutions of various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10,000 μM). The results showed that G-CQDs had a better quenching effect on picric acid and higher fluorescence intensity than mPD-CQDs. This indicates erythromycin played an important role in G-CQDs synthesis, enhancing their picric acid detection performance.

More precisely, it is important to determine if the fluorescence response intensity of G-CQDs and the PA concentration are correlated. The fluorescence intensity at the optimal excitation wavelength corresponding to the optimal emission wavelength was recorded after we sequentially added PA solutions with varying concentrations (0–1000 μM) configured in advance into the diluted G-CQDs under ideal experimental conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the fluorescence intensity of the G-CQDs gradually decreased as the concentration of PA steadily increased from 0 to 1000 μM, while the position of the optical emission peak remained unchanged. The fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(b), demonstrated a strong linear connection with the concentration of PA solution in the range of 0–60 μM. The linear fitting equation was y = 0.00218× + 1.00467 (R2 = 0.99216). The linear regression equation produced allowed for the quantitative determination of the concentration of PA within a certain range, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.18 μM. The comparison between this study and previous works in terms of synthesis materials, synthesis methods, linear range, and detection limit is shown in Table S1. Compared to previous studies, CQDs in this research have a lower detection limit. The addition of varying concentrations of PA to the diluted G-CQDs solution under daylight causes the color of the solution to change from yellowish to orange, as shown in Fig. 4(c). At the same time, the fluorescence color of the diluted G-CQDs solution under UV light changes from bright green to dark in Fig. 4(d). The naked eye can immediately detect PA by the color shift of G-CQDs' fluorescence. The easy and convenient measurements increase the detection range.

The possible mechanism of picric acid (PA) quenching the fluorescence of G-CQDs is through the inner filter effect (IFE) and the electrostatic interaction between G-CQDs and PA (Scheme 2 ) [55]. To investigate the fluorescence bursting principle by the addition of PA to G-CQDs. Fig. S6(a) illustrates that G-CQDs functioned as donor fluorophores and PA as acceptor chromophores following the addition of PA solution [56]. Additionally, no additional UV absorption peaks for G-CQDs were seen, indicating the formation of no new non-fluorescent complexes [57]. When the PA solution was added, as seen in Fig. S6(b), the ideal excitation and emission spectra of G-CQDs overlapped with the UV absorption spectra of PA. This resulted in an internal filtering effect of fluorescence and set off the fluorescence burst of G-CQDs [58]. In other words, PA will absorb the excitation and emission spectra of G-CQDs beforehand, which will increase the intensity of PA's absorption signal and diminish the fluorescence intensity signal of G-CQDs. Realization of G-CQDs' specific PA detection.

The surface charges of G-CQDs, PA, and G-CQDs + PA at different pH levels were analyzed using a nanoparticle analyzer (Zeta potential analyzer). As shown in Fig. S7, at pH 2, the zeta potential values of G-CQDs, PA, and G-CQDs + PA were −2.35 mV, +11.26 mV, and +4.15 mV, respectively. At pH 7, the values were −10.82 mV, +5.36 mV, and −6.52 mV, respectively, while at pH 12, they were −16.92 mV, +3.02 mV, and +10.73 mV, respectively. These data indicate that the electrostatic interactions between PA and G-CQDs vary under different pH conditions, thereby affecting the zeta potential of the mixed solution. This suggests that G-CQDs readily react with PA [59,60].

To achieve more convenient and intuitive PA detection, we prepared a fluorescent composite film by combining G-CQDs with PVP. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the film's color changed from light yellow to bright yellow with increasing PA concentration. Under UV irradiation, the fluorescence of the G-CQDs/PVA composite film also exhibited a visible change, transitioning from its initial green to black, enabling direct visual detection of PA. Fig. 5(b) demonstrates the stability of the G-CQDs/PVA composite film when stored at room temperature in an open environment. Even after 20 days, the film maintained stable fluorescent properties, showing only a slight darkening of its color under UV illumination. This confirms its excellent stability for practical applications and reliable long-term performance. Smartphone-based fluorescence sensors, with their inherent advantages of portability, flexibility, low cost, ease of operation, and real-time detection, have emerged as powerful analytical tools for diverse applications. Building on this platform, we developed G-CQDs/PVA fluorescent composite films to enable rapid sample detection. As shown in Fig. 5(c), fluorescence images of the G-CQDs were captured using a smartphone camera under different PA concentrations (0–100 μM). By employing a color selector and image processing application, the system automatically extracts fluorescence image features and converts them into machine-readable RGB values, which serve as quantitative parameters for PA detection. Fig. 5(d) demonstrates that the RGB values decrease linearly with increasing PA concentration. Within the range of 0–100 μM, the RGB values exhibit a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.99424) with PA concentration, following the equation: y = −1.81x + 523.591. These results confirm that the G-CQDs/PVA composite film, when integrated with smartphone technology, provides a practical, efficient, and portable solution for on-site quantitative PA monitoring.

We evaluated the detection performance of G-CQDs in real water samples (including South Lake water, tap water, and Yitong River industrial wastewater) through spike recovery experiments. The results demonstrated excellent accuracy and repeatability for domestic water samples: recovery rates were 98.52 %–101.90 % for South Lake water (average deviation: 1.16 %) and 97.83 %–102.70 % for tap water (average deviation: 1.09 %). For industrial wastewater samples from Yitong River, the recovery rates showed slightly greater variation (98.70 %–101.17 %) with an average deviation of 1.19 %, yet remained within acceptable limits. These findings confirm the reliability of the G-CQDs-based detection method across different water quality conditions (Table 1).

This study describes a one-step hydrothermal process that used erythromycin and m-phenylenediamine as precursors to create a kind of green fluorescence-emitting Carbon dots (G-CQDs) with a high quantum yield of 25.87 %. The G-CQDs demonstrated high photostability and resistance to salt, which had an average particle size of 6.75 nm. According to the internal filtration effect, PA bursts the fluorescence of G-CQDs, and the detection limit can be as low as 0.18 μM. The combination of G-CQDs with polyvinyl alcohol, aided by a smartphone, provides a reliable and convenient method for the rapid and accurate on-site detection of PA. This offers a viable substitute for detecting PA in actual water samples.

Caihong Mao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Haichao Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Meng Zhou: Visualization, Investigation. Miaomiao Chen: Visualization. Chunchao Wu: Supervision, Resources. Han Zuo: Supervision, Resources. Zhe Zhang: Validation, Resources, Investigation. Shujie Pang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xudong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52273122), the Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Jilin Province (Grant No. YDZJ202301ZYTS542).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112495.
